body:
## Summary

Both CLIs' `--capture browser` path uses a **time-based** wait to decide when `DOCS_modelChunk` data is \"ready\" for extraction. This is a heuristic, not correctness:

- **Rust** returns on the **first non-empty poll** → observable flake: large docs with >1 model chunk sometimes return a truncated capture (only the first chunk).
- **JS** waits a **flat 8000 ms** after `domcontentloaded` → works for docs that finish streaming within 8 s but has no guarantee for larger docs.

The right design is \"**poll until chunks stop changing**\" — i.e. quiescence detection with a configurable stability window and a hard timeout. Both CLIs should converge on that design.

## Observed flake (Rust)

Reinstalled Rust `0.3.8` in isolation, ran `--capture browser` against the same Google Doc 5 times back-to-back. The doc has enough content to be streamed across multiple `DOCS_modelChunk` assignments (the editor emits chunks progressively for bigger documents — size matters here, not content).

```
run 1: chunks=1 cid_urls=39 blocks=26 images=15 text_bytes=6489   # TRUNCATED
run 2: chunks=2 cid_urls=39 blocks=66 images=39 text_bytes=11553  # complete
run 3: chunks=1 cid_urls=39 blocks=26 images=15 text_bytes=6489   # TRUNCATED
run 4: chunks=1 cid_urls=39 blocks=26 images=15 text_bytes=6489   # TRUNCATED
run 5: chunks=2 cid_urls=39 blocks=66 images=39 text_bytes=11553  # complete
```

The image map (`cid_urls=39`) loads fully in both variants — only the `DOCS_modelChunk` stream is affected. When the capture is truncated, **more than half the document content is silently missing** from the archive (`document.md`, `document.html`, and `images/`).

JS `1.7.16` in isolation on the same doc returned 39 images / 280 markdown lines **5 times out of 5** — so on documents that finish streaming well under 8 s, JS's fixed wait happens to cover the race. On a doc that takes >8 s it would fail the same way.

## Root-cause pointers

**Rust — `rust/src/gdocs.rs`, `wait_for_google_docs_model_chunks` (~L1035)**:

```rust
while started.elapsed() < GDOCS_EDITOR_MODEL_WAIT {  // 30 s cap
    let model_data = ...;  // Runtime.evaluate → read window.__captured_chunks
    if !model_data.chunks.is_empty() {
        return Ok(model_data);  // <-- returns on FIRST non-empty, not on quiescence
    }
    tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_millis(250)).await;
}
```

Every 250 ms the loop asks the page \"how many chunks do you have?\" and returns the moment the answer is ≥1. If Google Docs emits chunk 1 at t≈1.2 s and chunk 2 at t≈1.8 s, the 1.25 s poll will happily return with just chunk 1.

**JS — `js/src/gdocs.js`, `captureGoogleDocWithBrowser` (~L413)**:

```js
await page.goto(editUrl, { waitUntil: 'domcontentloaded', timeout: 60000 });
await waitForPage(page, waitMs);   // waitMs defaults to 8000 ms — FLAT SLEEP
const modelData = await evaluateOnPage(page, () => {
  const chunks = [...(window.__captured_chunks || [])];
  ...
});
```

The `Object.defineProperty(window, 'DOCS_modelChunk', { set })` hook installed at L523 keeps recording chunks throughout the 8 s sleep, so late chunks *are* captured — but only if they arrive within 8 s. There is no verification that the stream has settled; a slow network or a big doc will silently lose tail chunks.

## Proposed fix (both CLIs — same algorithm)

Replace the time-based wait with a quiescence loop, parameterized by a **stability window** and a **hard timeout**:

```
stability_window = 1500 ms   (chunks must stop changing for this long)
hard_timeout     = 30000 ms  (give up if still churning)
poll_interval    = 250 ms    (how often to snapshot)
```

Algorithm (pseudocode, applies to both JS and Rust):

```
last_fingerprint = None
stable_since     = None
deadline         = now + hard_timeout
while now < deadline:
    fingerprint = (chunks.length, total_chunk_payload_size)
    if fingerprint == last_fingerprint and fingerprint.0 > 0:
        if stable_since is None: stable_since = now
        if now - stable_since >= stability_window: return chunks   # quiesced
    else:
        stable_since     = None
        last_fingerprint = fingerprint
    sleep(poll_interval)
raise TimeoutError(\"DOCS_modelChunk stream did not quiesce within hard_timeout\")
```

Notes:

- `total_chunk_payload_size` in the fingerprint catches the edge case where chunk count stays the same but an existing chunk's contents grow (observed occasionally — Docs appends to the same array). Just counting chunks isn't sufficient.
- The `fingerprint.0 > 0` guard prevents declaring quiescence before the first chunk arrives.
- `stability_window` of 1.5 s is a starting point — inspired by what a human \"looks idle\" threshold would be. Make it env-configurable (`WEB_CAPTURE_GDOCS_STABILITY_MS`) so large/slow docs can bump it.
- `hard_timeout` of 30 s matches Rust's current `GDOCS_EDITOR_MODEL_WAIT`. Keep it env-configurable too (`WEB_CAPTURE_GDOCS_MAX_WAIT_MS`).
- The return must never happen purely from elapsed time; the only success path is \"no changes for `stability_window`\". A timeout return should either error (strict mode) or return with a warning log (\"returned possibly-incomplete capture; set WEB_CAPTURE_GDOCS_MAX_WAIT_MS higher\").

## Why this also removes JS's hidden flake

JS's current 8000 ms wait works for the docs in existing test fixtures, but that's coincidence. On a slow network, an 8 s wait after `domcontentloaded` may also return before chunk streaming finishes. Once both CLIs adopt quiescence-based waiting, behavior becomes deterministic across network speed, doc size, and machine load — small docs return in ~1.5 s, large ones take as long as they take, nothing is silently truncated.

## Testing

Add a deterministic regression test that **forces non-trivial chunking** without depending on a specific private document:

1. **Synthetic public fixture.** Create a public Google Doc in CI's fixture set where the body is large enough to reliably emit >1 `DOCS_modelChunk`. A practical threshold is ~8-12 KB of document text: enough paragraphs of Lorem ipsum, a handful of inline images (use `docs.google.com/`-hosted test pngs), no unusual formatting required. The exact cutoff Google uses for chunking is implementation-defined, but in our testing docs with `text_bytes >= 10000` reliably emit ≥2 chunks.

2. **Two assertions in `gdocs_public_doc` live tests** (both languages):
   - `chunks >= 2` (or `text_bytes >= 10000`) — forces us to actually exercise the multi-chunk path.
   - **Stability check**: run capture N times (N=5 is enough); **image count and `text_bytes` must be identical across all runs**. This is the regression guard — if a run truncates, it'll stand out in the N-sample comparison.

3. **Log the stability diagnostic.** The `parsed Google Docs editor model` info line already prints `chunks`, `cid_urls`, `blocks`, `images`, `text_bytes`. Add `poll_count` and `stable_for_ms` to it so that operational issues (\"this doc took 14 s to quiesce\") are visible in production logs.

4. **No changes needed to existing test fixtures** — the issue-90 reference doc (`1f5zI2xOFpKa90v0GjamO_t7lqSdzMlaM`) is small and emits `chunks=1`, which is why it didn't surface this bug. Keep it; add the new multi-chunk fixture alongside.

## Repro recipe (without private docs)

1. Go to docs.google.com and create a new document.
2. Paste Lorem ipsum until `Tools → Word count → Characters` shows ≥ 10000.
3. Insert 4-6 `Insert → Image → Upload` blocks scattered through the doc.
4. `Share → Anyone with the link → Viewer`. Copy the link.
5. Put that URL in `$URL`, then:

```bash
# Rust
cargo install web-capture
for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do
  rm -rf run$i && mkdir run$i && cd run$i
  web-capture \"$URL\" --archive zip --capture browser 2>&1 \\
    | grep \"parsed Google Docs editor model\"
  cd ..
done
cargo uninstall web-capture
```

Expect: most runs show `chunks=2, images=<N>, text_bytes=<big>`, but a fraction show `chunks=1, images=<~half>, text_bytes=<~half>`. The fraction depends on machine speed and network — on a fast Mac (M-series, good connection) we saw 3/5 truncated runs.

```bash
# JS (for contrast — no flake on sub-8-s docs, but same design flaw)
npm i -g @link-assistant/web-capture
for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do
  rm -rf run$i && mkdir run$i && cd run$i
  web-capture \"$URL\" --archive zip --capture browser > /dev/null 2>&1
  unzip -p \"$(find . -name document.zip)\" document.md | wc -l
  cd ..
done
npm uninstall -g @link-assistant/web-capture
```

JS is likely to come out stable at 8 s, but increase doc size to ≥ 30-40 KB text to push past the 8 s window and you'll see it truncate too.

## Environment

- JS: `@link-assistant/web-capture@1.7.16` on Node 20.19.4 (package declares `node >=22 <23`).
- Rust: `web-capture 0.3.8` (crates.io), stable toolchain.
- Each CLI installed in full isolation (other uninstalled first) to rule out PATH shadowing.
- macOS 24.6.0.

## Related

- #104 (merged) — fixed HTML rendering parity; exposed this underlying capture-completeness bug, because divergence caused by this flake was previously attributed to the rendering difference.
- #102 — `--capture api` fidelity defects (separate issue).

comments.body:
We need to download all logs and data related about the issue to this repository, make sure we compile that data to `./docs/case-studies/issue-{id}` folder, and use it to do deep case study analysis (also make sure to search online for additional facts and data), in which we will reconstruct timeline/sequence of events, list of each and all requirements from the issue, find root causes of the each problem, and propose possible solutions and solution plans for each requirement (we should also check known existing components/libraries, that solve similar problem or can help in solutions).

If there is not enough data to find actual root cause, add debug output and verbose mode if not present, that will allow us to find root cause on next iteration.

If issue related to any other repository/project, where we can report issues on GitHub, please do so. Each issue must contain reproducible examples, workarounds and suggestions for fix the issue in code.

title: --capture browser: flaky / potentially incomplete captures — neither CLI waits for DOCS_modelChunk stream to quiesce
